chunk_summary: This section discusses the evaluation and management of non-response to nutrition therapy in children, including timelines for expected weight changes, adherence checks, and escalation to investigate organic causes or refer for specialist care.
learning_objectives: Understand the expected timeline for weight changes after initiating nutrition therapy in children.
Identify key factors to assess when evaluating non-response to nutrition interventions.
Recognize clinical red flags that warrant escalation or referral in cases of growth faltering.
Learn the first-line workup for common organic causes of non-response in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Weight gain should be measurable within 2–4 weeks of improved meal frequency and energy density in cases of uncomplicated dietary insufficiency.
Red flags for escalation include weight-for-length/height < -3 SD, bilateral pedal edema, recurrent pneumonia, chronic diarrhea (>14 days), and developmental regression.
Systematic screening for organic causes includes chronic infections (e.g., TB, HIV), celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, renal disease, congenital heart disease, and endocrine disorders.
A pragmatic first-line workup includes complete blood count, urinalysis, stool exam (occult blood/parasites), and targeted tests like tissue transglutaminase IgA for suspected celiac disease.
Referral should not be delayed if the child is clinically unwell, dehydrated, hypoxic, has edema, or if there are safeguarding concerns.
reference_quote: "In uncomplicated dietary insufficiency, improved meal frequency and energy density should produce measurable weight change within 2–4 weeks." (NICE NG75, 2017)
document_key_topics: Non-response to nutrition therapy in children
Timelines for weight change after dietary interventions
Clinical red flags and escalation criteria
First-line workup for organic causes of growth faltering
